skill.name: subagent-driven-development
skill.description: Coordinate independent implementation tasks through fresh subagents, spec compliance review, and code quality review in one development session.
skill.summary: Runs a task-by-task subagent workflow with review gates for implementation plans.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: AI review dismissed the high static hits as false positives caused by Markdown prompt templates, SHA labels, and checklist wording. The skill contains no executable scripts or network calls, but its intended workflow directs agents to inspect repository files, implement changes, and commit work. Publish with a warning for use only on trusted plans and repositories.

medium_findings.title: Agent Workflow Can Modify and Commit Repository Code
medium_findings.description: The workflow instructs implementer subagents to implement task requirements, write tests, verify behavior, and commit their work. This is expected for the skill, but it creates elevated risk when used with untrusted plans or repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly instruct implementation, testing, fixing, and committing. This is legitimate workflow behavior, so the concern is operational risk rather than malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Reviewer Subagents Inspect Repository Contents
medium_findings.description: Reviewer templates tell subagents to read actual code and compare implementation details with requirements. This filesystem access is necessary for review, but repository contents may include sensitive or private files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-reading behavior is explicit and central to the review workflow. The risk depends on repository sensitivity, so confidence is high for access scope but lower for harm.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fence and Weak Crypto Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby backtick and weak crypto hits occur in Markdown fences, prompt labels, or SHA placeholders used for code review context. No executable Ruby, shell backtick execution, or cryptographic implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is Markdown documentation and prompt template text, not executable code. The weak crypto hits align with words such as DESCRIPTION or SHA labels, not crypto logic.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Matches Are Review Checklist False Positives
low_findings.description: The system and network reconnaissance hits are questions about completeness, overbuilding, and code inspection. They do not instruct host enumeration, scanning, or network probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text is a code review checklist. No commands, network targets, or host discovery actions are present in the cited lines.

low_findings.title: Home Directory Path Is an Example, Not Hidden File Abuse
low_findings.description: The ~/.config/superpowers/hooks/ path appears in an example answer about user-level hook installation. It does not read, write, or conceal data by itself, but hook-related plans should still be reviewed before execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path is inside an example conversation and not part of executable code. The only residual concern is that future hook-installation tasks can affect user configuration.

content.value_statement: Large implementation plans can drift when one agent keeps all context. This skill splits work into fresh subagents and adds review gates after each task.
actual_capabilities: Breaks an implementation plan into independent task dispatches.
Provides prompt templates for implementer, spec reviewer, and code quality reviewer subagents.
Requires spec compliance review before code quality review.
Guides review loops until issues are fixed and rechecked.
Uses TodoWrite-style task tracking for same-session progress.
Supports final code review after all planned tasks are complete.
limitations: Works best when tasks are mostly independent.
Requires a clear implementation plan before use.
Can use many subagent calls for each task.
Does not provide executable automation or safety controls.

use_cases.title: Execute Feature Plans
use_cases.description: Run a multi-task feature plan with separate implementer and reviewer passes for each task.

use_cases.title: Control Scope During Delivery
use_cases.description: Check that each task matches the written requirements before quality review begins.

use_cases.title: Improve Review Discipline
use_cases.description: Add repeatable review loops for teams that want stricter implementation checkpoints.

prompt_templates.title: Run One Planned Task
prompt_templates.prompt: Use subagent-driven-development for Task 1 in my plan. Dispatch one implementer, then run spec and quality review before marking it done.

prompt_templates.title: Execute a Small Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use subagent-driven-development to execute this three-task plan. Keep tasks sequential, answer subagent questions, and require both reviews for each task.

prompt_templates.title: Recover From Review Issues
prompt_templates.prompt: Use subagent-driven-development for the next task. If either reviewer finds issues, send them back to the implementer and repeat review until approved.

prompt_templates.title: Complete a Full Branch
prompt_templates.prompt: Use subagent-driven-development for the full implementation plan. Track every task, preserve review order, collect git SHAs, and run final review before completion.

output_examples.input: A five-task feature plan needs implementation in the current session.
output_examples.output: The assistant extracts each task, dispatches a fresh implementer, runs spec review, runs quality review, and marks each task complete only after approval.

output_examples.input: The spec reviewer reports that one requested behavior is missing.
output_examples.output: The assistant sends the issue back to the implementer, waits for the fix, and runs the spec review again before quality review.

output_examples.input: All planned tasks are complete and individually reviewed.
output_examples.output: The assistant dispatches a final code review for the complete implementation and reports readiness for branch finishing.

best_practices: Use this skill only with trusted implementation plans and repositories.
Provide full task text to each subagent instead of making it search for context.
Keep the required review order: spec compliance first, code quality second.
anti_patterns: Do not dispatch multiple implementers on overlapping files.
Do not skip re-review after a reviewer finds issues.
Do not use this workflow when requirements are still unclear.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It coordinates implementation tasks through fresh subagents and requires two review stages after each task.

faq.question: Does it include executable code?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown workflow with prompt templates, not a script or package.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it guides agents to inspect files, change code, and commit work.

faq.question: When should I avoid it?
faq.answer: Avoid it when tasks are tightly coupled, requirements are unclear, or the repository is untrusted.

faq.question: Can it run tasks in parallel?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflow warns against parallel implementation subagents because file conflicts can occur.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
